skill.name: github-cli
skill.description: Guides assistants to use GitHub CLI for pull request, issue, review, CI, and repository context gathering.
skill.summary: Use GitHub CLI to collect PR, issue, review, CI, and repository context before acting.
skill.author: BfdCampos

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many external command references, two hardcoded GitHub URLs, and five weak cryptography patterns. Review confirms the command and network patterns are expected for a GitHub CLI context skill, while the weak cryptography alerts are false positives. No evidence found of prompt injection, malicious intent, hidden exfiltration, or destructive GitHub operations.

medium_findings.title: External GitHub CLI Command Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill repeatedly instructs the assistant to run gh commands for pull requests, issues, repository data, and CI checks. This is expected for the skill purpose, but it can execute local commands and reveal private GitHub context available to the authenticated user.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command references are explicit and numerous, and the semantic context confirms legitimate GitHub CLI use. The risk is operational rather than malicious because the examples use standard read-oriented gh commands and placeholders.

medium_findings.title: Authenticated GitHub Network Access
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends gh api and GitHub URL workflows that send requests to GitHub using local authentication. This is normal for a GitHub context skill, but users should understand that private repository metadata, comments, and CI information may be retrieved into the assistant session.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The network behavior follows directly from gh api commands and GitHub URLs. No evidence found of non-GitHub endpoints, hidden data exfiltration, or unrelated network destinations.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The static analyzer flagged weak cryptographic algorithm patterns, but the referenced lines contain prose, headings, or gh command comments. No evidence found of hashing, encryption, crypto API use, or security-sensitive cryptographic logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged lines were manually reviewed and do not contain cryptographic operations. The alerts appear to be substring matches within ordinary documentation text.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded GitHub Example URLs
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are examples showing how to convert GitHub pull request and issue links into gh commands. They point to github.com example repository paths and do not indicate suspicious network behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are plainly introduced as examples and are immediately followed by equivalent gh commands. No evidence found that they are used for tracking, callbacks, or exfiltration.

dangerous_patterns.title: External GitHub CLI Command Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill repeatedly instructs the assistant to run gh commands for pull requests, issues, repository data, and CI checks. This is expected for the skill purpose, but it can execute local commands and reveal private GitHub context available to the authenticated user.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command references are explicit and numerous, and the semantic context confirms legitimate GitHub CLI use. The risk is operational rather than malicious because the examples use standard read-oriented gh commands and placeholders.

dangerous_patterns.title: Authenticated GitHub Network Access
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill recommends gh api and GitHub URL workflows that send requests to GitHub using local authentication. This is normal for a GitHub context skill, but users should understand that private repository metadata, comments, and CI information may be retrieved into the assistant session.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The network behavior follows directly from gh api commands and GitHub URLs. No evidence found of non-GitHub endpoints, hidden data exfiltration, or unrelated network destinations.

content.value_statement: GitHub work often needs PR comments, issue discussion, CI status, and repository metadata. This skill guides assistants to use GitHub CLI commands to collect that context before making decisions.
actual_capabilities: Recommends gh pr commands for viewing pull requests, diffs, comments, reviews, checks, and lists.
Shows gh issue commands for viewing issues, listing issues, searching, and reading comments.
Uses gh repo, gh release, gh run, and gh api examples for repository context.
Converts GitHub pull request and issue URLs into gh commands with explicit repository targets.
Combines local git history with GitHub discussion context for fuller review preparation.
Suggests --json output and gh api for structured or lower-level GitHub data access.
limitations: Requires GitHub CLI to be installed and authenticated in the local environment.
Can only access repositories and data permitted by the active GitHub credentials.
Provides guidance and command examples, not a standalone automation script.
Focuses on reading GitHub context and does not define write or mutation workflows.

use_cases.title: Prepare for Pull Request Review
use_cases.description: Collect PR descriptions, diffs, CI checks, comments, and review history before giving feedback.

use_cases.title: Investigate Issue Context
use_cases.description: Review issue details, discussion, labels, and related repository data before proposing a fix.

use_cases.title: Understand Repository Activity
use_cases.description: Inspect branches, releases, workflow runs, and open collaboration items during project triage.

prompt_templates.title: Check a Pull Request
prompt_templates.prompt: Use GitHub CLI to inspect pull request NUMBER in this repository. Summarize the description, status, comments, reviews, and CI checks.

prompt_templates.title: Investigate an Issue
prompt_templates.prompt: Use gh to review issue NUMBER and its comments. Identify the problem, current discussion, labels, and likely next action.

prompt_templates.title: Review a GitHub Link
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the provided GitHub URL with gh and the correct --repo value. Gather relevant context before recommending changes.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Review Brief
prompt_templates.prompt: Combine local git history with gh PR details, diff, checks, comments, and reviews. Produce a concise review brief with risks and open questions.

output_examples.input: Review pull request 123 before I comment.
output_examples.output: A concise brief covering PR purpose, changed areas, CI state, reviewer concerns, unresolved comments, and suggested review focus.

output_examples.input: Look into issue 456 and tell me what is blocking it.
output_examples.output: A triage summary with the issue goal, key discussion points, labels, participants, likely blocker, and recommended next step.

output_examples.input: This GitHub link points to a failing workflow run. Explain what happened.
output_examples.output: A workflow context summary with run status, relevant repository target, recent failures, and next diagnostic checks.

best_practices: Confirm the repository target before running gh commands outside the current checkout.
Start with read-only context commands before making recommendations or edits.
Use structured gh output when the assistant must compare fields or summarize many records.
anti_patterns: Do not rely only on local git history when PR discussion or CI status matters.
Do not run gh commands against ambiguous repositories without specifying the owner and repo.
Do not expose private GitHub context unless the user requested that repository information.

faq.question: Does this skill require GitHub CLI?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance depends on the gh command being installed and authenticated.

faq.question: Can it read private repositories?
faq.answer: It can read private repository data only when the active GitHub credentials allow access.

faq.question: Does it create or change GitHub data?
faq.answer: The documented examples focus on reading context. They do not define mutation workflows.

faq.question: Why use gh instead of only git?
faq.answer: Git shows commits and branches. gh can also show PR discussion, issue comments, reviews, and CI status.

faq.question: Can it handle GitHub URLs?
faq.answer: Yes. It explains how to extract repository, pull request, and issue information from GitHub URLs.

faq.question: Is the skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: It is publishable with a medium risk warning because it encourages external commands and authenticated GitHub network access.
